channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Irvine Wanted a New Concert Venue. But Not on Live Nation’s Terms.
item.description: As the company’s antitrust trial continues, one incident involving an amphitheater shows how it clashed not with another industry titan, but a small city.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Irvine, a city of 300,000 about an hour’s drive south of downtown Los Angeles, was hoping to work with Live Nation to replace a popular amphitheater that shut down in 2016.

item.title: Review: ‘Innocence’ Tackles School Shootings at the Met Opera
item.description: Kaija Saariaho’s “Innocence,” a powerful and kaleidoscopic depiction of mass violence and its aftermath, has arrived at the Metropolitan Opera.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Richard Termine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Simon Stone’s realistic production of “Innocence” opened at the Metropolitan Opera on Monday night.

item.title: Duchamp Made a Urinal Into Art in 1917. We’re Still Discussing It.
item.description: Marcel Duchamp changed the face of culture in the 20th century, and beyond, with an unconventional sculpture that challenged how we think of art.
item.dc:creator: Blake Gopnik

item.title: Protests in Mexico Challenge Move of Frida Kahlo Trove to Spain
item.description: Officials scrambled to reassure Mexicans that a collection of esteemed artworks would return by 2028. A rarely-seen will may clarify the collector’s wishes.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Burnett and Zachary Small
item.category: Museum of Modern Art (Mexico City, Mexico)

item.title: At Broadway’s ‘Rocky Horror Show,’ Fan Participation Is Tricky
item.description: The new musical is trying to calibrate just how much to rein in the audience participation that longtime fans are used to.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman and Graham Dickie
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie for The New York Times
item.media:description: Naomi Lafont, in face paint inspired by a dance scene in the film version, is one of may audience members dressing up for the new Broadway revival of “The Rocky Horror Show.”

item.title: Presto! In Chicago, a High-End Magic Palace Appears
item.description: The entrepreneur Glen Tullman is betting people want to dress up and watch magicians in a luxury setting. Either it will work or $50 million will go poof.
item.dc:creator: David Segal and Evan Jenkins
item.category: Hand and the Eye, The (Metamorphosis Chicago LLC)
item.media:description: Glen Tullman, the health care entrepreneur bankrolling The Hand and the Eye, has been a magic fan his entire life.

item.title: ‘Becky Shaw’ Review: Funny vs. Likable? Funny Wins.
item.description: Alden Ehrenreich makes a show-stealing Broadway debut in Gina Gionfriddo’s comedy about two old friends, one disastrous blind date and the dicey aftermath.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times
item.media:description: In “Becky Shaw,” the characters played by Madeline Brewer, left, and Alden Ehrenreich are set up on a blind date.

item.title: Wireless Festival Canceled After U.K. Blocks Kanye West From Entering Country
item.description: Entry to the country is being denied to the rapper formerly known as Kanye West because his presence “would not be conducive to the public good.” The festival he was due to headline is now canceled.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Evan Agostini/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Ye, the rapper formerly known as Kanye West, had made a last-minute plea on Tuesday in which he offered to meet with Jews in Britain.

item.title: Netflix Explores a Buzzy Chess Cheating Scandal
item.description: “Untold,” a sports documentary series, recounts a notorious match between two grandmasters and the cheating accusations that rocked the chess world and beyond.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Loeb McClain

item.title: How Duchamp Inspired These 4 Artists
item.description: Decades after his death, he continues to influence contemporary artists. Here are four — of many — who have riffed on his urinal sculpture “Fountain.”
item.dc:creator: Blake Gopnik

item.title: Justice Dept. Resolves Broadway Touring Company Investigation
item.description: Broadway Across America will not be prosecuted after acknowledging it signed a noncompete agreement with another presenter, the agency said.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch and Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Robert Alexander/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Majestic Theater in San Antonio in 2018. The Justice Department focused on an agreement between Broadway Across America and a company that managed shows in San Antonio.

item.title: How Did Black Music Take Over the World? Let Melvin Gibbs Explain.
item.description: Since the late ’70s, the bassist has worked to map a musical route that mirrored the trans-Atlantic slave trade and birthed nearly all of American popular music.
item.dc:creator: Hank Shteamer
item.category: How Black Music Took Over the World (Book)
item.media:credit: Galen Fletcher for The New York Times
item.media:description: Melvin Gibbs has become one of his generation’s most admired and least easily categorized instrumentalists and conceptualists.

item.title: Is This Bach’s Most Underrated Music?
item.description: The sprawling organ collection “Clavier-Übung III” is not as widely known as it should be. An excellent new recording could change that.
item.dc:creator: David Allen
item.media:credit: Sam Dearden for The New York Times
item.media:description: The English organist James McVinnie said that Bach’s collection “Clavier-Übung III” is still very relevant today.

item.title: Europe’s Museums Confront the (Literal) Skeletons in Their Closets
item.description: Institutions are grappling with the human remains in their collections that were used to justify debunked theories about race.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal
item.media:credit: Ilvy Njiokiktjien for The New York Times

item.title: Late Night Puzzles Over Trump’s ‘Mixed Message’ on Iran
item.description: “Everyone, most notably the people of Iran, were wondering if their civilization was going to die tonight. Well, good news, it didn’t,” Jimmy Kimmel said after a cease-fire was announced.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Fanciful and Fabulous: ‘Cats: The Jellicle Ball’ on Broadway
item.description: For their 10th life, the cats strut and duckwalk in a reappraisal of Andrew Lloyd Webber’s 1981 musical, which has shifted to the queer ballroom scene.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw and Sara Krulwich
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times

item.title: Taylor Frankie Paul Files Competing Court Petition Against Dakota Mortensen
item.description: The reality star and her former boyfriend, Dakota Mortensen, have filed dueling court petitions that paint each other as the main aggressor in a recent altercation.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives (TV Program)

item.title: Nathan Farb’s Roving Lens
item.description: An immersion in the wide-ranging work of a photographer who has died at 85.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Nathan Farb, 85, Dies; Photographed Hippies, Siberians and Mountains
item.description: His camera captured subjects as diverse as New York City during the Summer of Love in 1967, Siberia under Soviet rule and the Adirondacks in upstate New York.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: Peter Schrag Dies at 94; Wrote of Dangers of California’s Populist Streak
item.description: His best-received book explored the state’s infatuation with voter initiatives, which were sometimes pushed with anti-immigrant fervor.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.category: Paradise Lost: California's Experience, America's Future (Book)

item.title: 9 Ethereal, Emotional, Chaotic and Goofy Cloud-Rap Gems
item.description: A quick guide to the underground genre, featuring fakemink, Bladee, Yung Lean and more.
item.dc:creator: Dani Blum
item.media:credit: Victor Boyko/Getty Images for Puma
item.media:description: The British rapper fakemink has staying power.

item.title: Joe Conzo Sr., Guardian of Tito Puente’s Legacy, Dies at 83
item.description: He went from being a fan of the “Mambo King” to becoming his manager and personal historian. Later, he kept alive memories of a bygone era of New York Latino culture.
item.dc:creator: Alex Traub

item.title: Bill Ackman’s Pershing Square Bids to Buy Universal Music Group
item.description: The complex transaction pitched by the billionaire hedge fund manager would need to pass muster with the music giant’s shareholders.
item.dc:creator: Michael J. de la Merced and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Cassidy Araiza for The New York Times
item.media:description: Taylor Swift is a member of the Universal Music Group roster, along with Bad Bunny, Kendrick Lamar, Coldplay and others.

item.title: A Brief History of 4 Urinals
item.description: Marcel Duchamp’s original “Fountain” sculpture vanished within days of its 1917 appearance. He later introduced these versions in response to demand.
item.dc:creator: Blake Gopnik and Daniel Arnold
item.media:credit: Association Marcel Duchamp/ADAGP, Paris/Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York
item.media:description: In 1963, Duchamp gave permission for a critic in Stockholm to recreate his 1917 urinal using a restaurant’s model as a stand-in.

item.title: “Cats: The Jellicle Ball” Live From the New York Times Office
item.description: Five of the show’s stars strutted and prowled amid the desks of T Magazine.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Finding Trends in Sports Stats and ‘Wheel of Fortune’ Puzzles
item.description: Ben Blatt, a data reporter for The Upshot, dreams up inventive ways to answer questions like: “Is Taylor Swift actually increasing N.F.L. ratings?”
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Stephen Colbert Mocks Trump’s Expletive-Filled Post Threatening Iran
item.description: Late night hosts mocked the president’s expletive-filled ultimatum to Iran, posted on Easter Sunday morning.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: In Paris’s Catacombs, Can a Restoration Breathe New Life Into City’s Dead?
item.description: For centuries, the bones of some six million people were buried in the catacombs beneath the city. Curators are trying to preserve and modernize the tunnels while maintaining the spooky ambience.
item.dc:creator: Ana Castelain
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Workers restoring the catacombs in Paris last month.

item.title: Ronald H. Spector, Who Traced Social History in Books on War, Dies at 83
item.description: A Vietnam veteran-turned-academic historian, he drew acclaim for portraying conflicts from the perspectives of generals as well as grunts on all sides, both in Vietnam and in World War II.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: Elisabeth Waldo, 107, Who Fused Indigenous and Western Music, Dies
item.description: A classically-trained violinist, she incorporated traditional instruments native to Latin America in Western-style scores to create an atmospheric hybrid.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter

item.title: In Savannah Guthrie’s Return to ‘Today,’ a Rare TV Example of How to Live With Not Knowing
item.description: With her mother still missing, the “Today” host’s comeback was a rare TV example of learning to live with not knowing.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik
item.media:credit: NBC, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Savannah Guthrie, right, with her “Today” colleague Jenna Bush Hager. Guthrie stepped outside the studio to greet well-wishers during her first episode back since the disappearance of her mother.

item.title: The Volcano Lover: An Anarchic Young Composer’s Masterpiece
item.description: Simon Hanes, a leading musician in New York’s downtown scene, has recorded his “Gargantua,” a work for 15 musicians that nods to volcanoes, Rabelais and Dante.
item.dc:creator: Walker Mimms
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times

item.title: Forget the A.I. Apocalypse. Memes Have Already Nuked Our Culture.
item.description: From our jokes and slang to the White House’s policy messaging, internet “brain rot” has escaped our phones to take over … well, everything.
item.dc:creator: Willy Staley

item.title: ‘Firebird’ Finds Its Wings Again at Dance Theater of Harlem
item.description: And so does the company, which revives its lush, fantastical, large-scale production, set in the Caribbean.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas and Marc J. Franklin

item.title: ‘Euphoria,’ Plus 8 Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: The HBO show returns after a long hiatus. And the March Madness final airs.
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dulany
item.media:credit: Eddy Chen/HBO
item.media:description: Zendaya returns as Rue in Season 3 of “Euphoria.”

item.title: ‘The Comeback’ Season 3, Episode 3: The Aww-Ick
item.description: This week’s episode suggests that Lisa Kudrow and Michael Patrick King don’t mind mixing in a little sentiment with their satire.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.media:credit: Erin Simkin/HBO
item.media:description: Lisa Kudrow in “The Comeback.”

item.title: On ‘S.N.L.,’ Bondi Boasts of Being ‘First Woman Ever Fired as Attorney General’
item.description: Ashley Padilla was both Pam Bondi and Kristi Noem on “Saturday Night Live,” with Tina Fey, Candice Bergen and Melissa McCarthy. Jack Black and Jack White were host and musical guest.
item.dc:creator: Dave Itzkoff

item.title: Despite Poor Reviews, ‘The Super Mario Galaxy Movie’ Is a Megahit
item.description: The animated sequel collected about $373 million worldwide over its first five days in theaters. “Kids love the movie,” a box office analyst said.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Nintendo and Illumination/Universal Pictures
item.media:description: “The Super Mario Galaxy Movie,” based on Nintendo characters, cost roughly $110 million to make.

item.title: ‘Hacks’ Has the Last Laugh
item.description: As the HBO comedy finished production earlier this year, a photographer captured some of the final days on set and the showrunners looked back on its award-winning run.
item.dc:creator: Chantal Anderson and Alexis Soloski
item.media:credit: Chantal Anderson for The New York Times

item.title: How Did You Feel About ‘The Drama’ Plot Twist? Tell Us Your Thoughts
item.description: The sort-of rom-com stars Zendaya and Robert Pattinson as an engaged couple whose relationship is upended by a revelation that has divided audiences.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: How Scientifically Accurate Is ‘Project Hail Mary’? Experts Weigh In
item.description: Based on hard science fiction, a genre that prioritizes scientific accuracy, the blockbuster gets a lot right but misses a few things, experts say.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski

item.title: U2, Anne Hathaway: 9 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: U2 surprises with an Easter EP, and Anne Hathaway puts out a convincingly delivered pop song that boasts FKA twigs as a writer.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Chung Sung-Jun/Getty Images
item.media:description: U2 has continued to follow a liturgical calendar with the surprise release of a new EP on Good Friday.

item.title: Regé-Jean Page on the Album He Considers Flawless
item.description: “It’s raw, real, sensitive, strong, vulnerable, righteous, romantic, genius,” said the “Bridgerton” actor, who stars in the new film “You, Me & Tuscany.”
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Chris Pizzello/Invision, via Associated Press

item.title: After 11 Years in Court, Heir Reclaims a Modigliani Looted by the Nazis
item.description: A judge ruled against a holding company controlled by David Nahmad, the billionaire art dealer, which had bought the work at auction in 1996.
item.dc:creator: Graham Bowley
item.media:credit: via Sotheby's
item.media:description: “Seated Man With a Cane,” painted by Amedeo Modigliani in 1918.

item.title: Carol Mullins, Who Illuminated Dance for Decades, Dies at 86
item.description: She knew nothing about lighting when the director Robert Wilson asked her to work on his shows. She then spent over 40 years as a designer for Danspace Project.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: Carol Mullins in 2024. In 1982, she got a call from the director of Danspace Project in Manhattan, offering her a position as the organization’s resident lighting designer. It was a job that Ms. Mullins would keep until her death.

item.title: Dolores Keane, Singer Known as the ‘Soul of Ireland,’ Dies at 72
item.description: She sang of homesickness and longing, and often collaborated with American musicians like John Prine and Emmylou Harris.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Mick Hutson/Redferns, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Dolores Keane performing in 1990. She emerged in the 1970s as part of a renaissance in the traditional music of Ireland.

item.title: ‘The Drama’ and the Secrets That Can Unravel a Relationship
item.description: In the new film, a shocking revelation upends the lives of a couple played by Zendaya and Robert Pattinson, raising the question: Are some betrayals simply unforgivable?
item.dc:creator: The Styles Desk

item.title: Chekhov Plays for an Un-Chekhov Time
item.description: The directors Michael DeFilippis, Dmitry Krymov and Aleksandr Molochnikov all infuse their current productions with a burning, modern rage.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: Pedro Friedeberg, Whose Hand Chair Drew Outsize Attention, Dies at 90
item.description: Known for his hallucinatory canvases, he cemented his fame with a chair, shaped like a hand, that was both a work of art and a pop culture curio.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: In ‘Burnout Paradise,’ Running on Treadmills Is Only the Half of It
item.description: In “Burnout Paradise,” four performers try ambitious tasks while jogging. If they can’t beat their run time goal, the audience gets its money back.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg and Yuvraj Khanna
item.media:credit: Yuvraj Khanna for The New York Times

item.title: Five International Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include a feminist coming-of-age tale from India, a documentary about grasshopper harvesting in Uganda, a queer fable from Brazil and more.
item.dc:creator: Devika Girish

item.title: Who Is the Polka-Dot Band That Plays Virtuosic Rock? Angine de Poitrine.
item.description: The virtuosic rock duo from Quebec releases its second album, “Vol. II,” this week.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles

item.title: On ‘Company Retreat,’ Anthony Norman Finished the Job
item.description: The unwitting hero of this Amazon prank comedy series acted heroically in the final episode.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Jamie Lee Taete
item.category: Jury Duty Presents: Company Retreat (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Jamie Lee Taete for The New York Times

item.title: Nadav Lapid’s Film ‘Yes’ Is Intended to Anger Both Israel’s Right and Left
item.description: The director Nadav Lapid uses his work to confront his discomfort with nationalism in his home country, never more so than in “Yes.”
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman

item.title: 8 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: What to Know About ‘The Testaments,’ a Sequel to Margaret Atwood’s ‘The Handmaid’s Tale’
item.description: “The Testaments” focuses on a younger generation coming of age inside Gilead, the religious regime first imagined in Margaret Atwood’s 1985 dystopian thriller.
item.dc:creator: Martine Thompson
item.category: The Testaments: The Sequel to The Handmaid's Tale (Book)

item.title: Seats Left Empty on Smithsonian Board as Strain With White House Persists
item.description: The terms of two governing members have expired, but their replacements have yet to be named as the institution faces President Trump’s effort to play a role in the selections.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Chip Somodevilla via Getty Images
item.media:description: The strains between the Smithsonian and the Trump administration can be seen in the delays in filling seats on its governing board.

item.title: ‘Jimmy & the Demons’ Review
item.description: The impish but thoughtful James Grashow, his sculpture and his long marriage are the subjects of Cindy Meehl’s film.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: Ben Lerner’s ‘Transcription’ Shows Us Why the Novel Will Never Die
item.description: With “Transcription,” the writer makes a case for the vitality of the form.
item.dc:creator: Parul Sehgal

item.title: Late Night Says Goodbye to Pam Bondi
item.description: President Trump is “on a bit of a firing spree,” Jimmy Fallon said on “The Tonight Show” on Thursday, adding, “Ironically, the only staffer who has immunity is RFK Jr.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
